countries.name: United States

key_capabilities: 11 carrier strike groups providing global power projection
F-22/F-35 stealth air superiority and deep strike
Precision munitions (JDAM, LRASM, Tomahawk) with 1000+ km reach
Special Operations Command (SOCOM) with global reach
Dominant cyber warfare and space assets (GPS, ISR satellites)
Nuclear triad with survivable second-strike capability
vulnerabilities: Long supply lines to any Middle East or Pacific theater
Casualty sensitivity in democratic society limits ground operations
Forward bases in Gulf and Pacific vulnerable to ballistic missile salvo
A2AD threats constrain carrier group operations within 1000km of peer adversary coast
Overextension across multiple simultaneous theaters degrades effectiveness in each
155mm shell production was 14,000/month in 2022 vs 20,000+/day needed for peer war

economy.energy_independence: Net exporter via shale (Permian Basin); no strategic energy vulnerability
economy.sanctions_resilience: Very high — controls SWIFT, dollar reserve currency, can impose rather than absorb sanctions
economy.war_financing_capacity: High short-term via deficit spending; constrained long-term by $34T+ debt and rising interest costs
economy.industrial_mobilization: Low — manufacturing offshored; defense industrial base hollowed during post-Cold War peace dividend

alliances: Israel special relationship ($3.8B annual military aid)
countries.notes: Dominant global military power structurally constrained by financialized economy and $34T+ debt. Reserve currency status provides unique war financing capacity. Historically retreats after 5-7 years of unpopular wars due to democratic fatigue (Vietnam: yr7; Afghanistan: yr20). Low manufacturing base is the decisive long-war vulnerability.

countries.name: Russia

key_capabilities: Largest nuclear arsenal globally — full triad with hypersonic delivery (Kinzhal, Zircon)
Layered strategic air defense: S-300/S-400/S-500, 3000+ SAM batteries
Artillery mass — 2000+ self-propelled guns, shell production reactivated to 3M/year
Electronic warfare dominance in contested European theater
Submarine fleet threatening NATO Atlantic supply lines
Hypersonic glide vehicles defeating current missile defense architectures
vulnerabilities: Precision-guided munition stockpile degraded; dependent on Iranian drone imports
Microelectronics sanctions limiting advanced weapons production
Officer casualty rates degrading command quality
Demographic decline: TFR 1.5, shrinking military-age cohort by 2030
Single-commodity (hydrocarbon) economy creates oil-price vulnerability

economy.energy_independence: Major exporter — oil, gas, grain, fertilizer; energy is primary economic weapon
economy.sanctions_resilience: Moderate-high — adapted after 2014 and 2022 via China/India trade pivot
economy.war_financing_capacity: Moderate — resource revenues sustain at $70+/barrel oil; National Wealth Fund depleting
economy.industrial_mobilization: High — Soviet-era defense industrial base partially reactivated; 3x shell production 2022-2024

countries.notes: Low debt and resource wealth provide genuine war-sustainability advantage over Western opponents. Higher manufacturing-to-FIRE ratio than US/UK means better industrial war production. Nuclear arsenal provides escalation dominance. Authoritarian structure tolerates casualties without electoral pressure — ~200,000 KIA/WIA absorbed with political stability (2024).

countries.name: China

key_capabilities: World's largest navy by hull count (370+ warships); shipbuilding capacity exceeds US
DF-21D/DF-26 carrier-killer missiles forcing US carriers 1000km+ from coast
DF-41 ICBM and DF-5B providing credible nuclear second-strike
Comprehensive A2AD in South/East China Sea across three island chains
World-leading hypersonic glide vehicle program (DF-17)
Cyber warfare and counter-space capabilities (ASAT tested 2007)
vulnerabilities: No significant combat experience since 1979 — untested operational command
Taiwan Strait amphibious invasion requires landing ship capacity currently insufficient
Malacca Strait dependency: 80% of oil imports transit this chokepoint
No deep-water navy combat tested against peer adversary
Political loyalty requirements may compromise military merit selection

economy.energy_independence: Net importer — Malacca vulnerability is existential strategic constraint
economy.sanctions_resilience: Moderate — large domestic market; technology dependency on Western semiconductors is acute
economy.war_financing_capacity: High — $3.2T forex reserves, extensive domestic bond market
economy.industrial_mobilization: Very high — world's largest manufacturing base; can scale munitions production rapidly
key_economic_weapons: Rare earth export controls (85% of global refining)

alliances: Belt and Road dependency network (~150 countries)
countries.notes: Industrial mobilization capacity is the decisive long-war advantage — can outproduce any adversary in conventional munitions within 2 years. Malacca dilemma is the one decisive strategic vulnerability: naval blockade could starve the economy within 6 months. Rare earth dominance ($160B/year global market) is an economic weapon with no short-term substitute.

countries.name: Iran

military_strength.nuclear_threshold: Approximately 1-2 weeks from weapons-grade enrichment to device assembly; 60% enrichment achieved
key_capabilities: Proxy network: Hezbollah (100,000+ rockets), Houthis (Yemen), PMF (Iraq), Hamas (Gaza)
Ballistic missile arsenal: 3,000+ missiles including Shahab-3 (2,000km) and Fattah-1 hypersonic
Drone swarm warfare: Shahed-136 kamikaze drones at $20,000/unit, mass-producible
Strait of Hormuz control via coastal missiles, mines, and fast attack craft (IRGC Navy)
Underground hardened facilities (Fordow) resistant to conventional bunker-busters
Asymmetric A2AD negating US carrier superiority within Persian Gulf
vulnerabilities: No air force parity — F-14s and F-4s from 1970s, severely degraded
Economic isolation limits advanced weapons procurement
Internal ethnic tensions (Kurds 10%, Arabs 3%, Baloch 2%) exploitable by adversaries
Command structure split between regular army and IRGC creates friction

economy.energy_independence: Major oil and gas exporter despite sanctions; 10% of global gas reserves
economy.sanctions_resilience: Very high — 45 years of adaptation; shadow economy via UAE, Turkey, China
economy.war_financing_capacity: Moderate — constrained by sanctions but low debt provides flexibility
economy.industrial_mobilization: Moderate — domestic arms industry (Shahed drones exported to Russia in thousands)
key_economic_weapons: Strait of Hormuz closure (20% of global oil supply)
Proxy cost imposition without direct engagement

alliances: Axis of Resistance: Hezbollah, Houthis, PMF, Palestinian Islamic Jihad
countries.notes: Proxy network is Iran's strategic multiplier. Strait of Hormuz closure would spike global oil 50%+ within 72 hours. 45-year sanctions adaptation makes economic pressure less effective than Western planners assume.

countries.name: Israel

key_capabilities: Iron Dome (90%+ intercept), David's Sling (medium), Arrow 2/3 (ballistic) — layered defense
F-35I Adir fleet with indigenous sensor suite
Mossad intelligence: human intelligence network across Arab world and Iran
Unit 8200 cyber warfare: Stuxnet-class offensive capability
Nuclear ambiguity — Samson Option provides existential deterrence
vulnerabilities: Small population (9.5M) cannot sustain multi-front attrition
Zero strategic depth: Tel Aviv is 15km from West Bank
Reserve mobilization requires 48-72 hours, vulnerable to surprise
Multi-front war simultaneously unsustainable
Internal political fragmentation weakens war cabinet decision-making

economy.energy_independence: Gas self-sufficient via Leviathan offshore field
economy.sanctions_resilience: High — US backing provides economic buffers
economy.war_financing_capacity: High short-term with US emergency aid; constrained without US support
economy.industrial_mobilization: Moderate — world-class precision defense electronics but small industrial base

alliances: United States (special relationship, $3.8B annual military aid)
Abraham Accords: UAE, Bahrain, Morocco, Sudan
India defense partnership (world's largest buyer of Israeli arms)
countries.notes: Qualitative military edge maintained by US technology transfers. Core strategic constraint: 9.5M population requires decisive short wars — every day of multi-front war increases existential risk.

countries.name: Saudi Arabia

key_capabilities: F-15SA advanced variant fleet with US munitions
Patriot PAC-3 air defense protecting oil infrastructure
ARAMCO oil infrastructure as economic lever (17% global reserves)
vulnerabilities: Yemen war revealed military limitations: 9 years, $100B+, Houthi threat unresolved
Abqaiq oil processing catastrophically vulnerable — 2019 drone attack proved this
No nuclear deterrent; entirely dependent on US security umbrella
Military performance reliant on Western contractors and mercenaries

economy.energy_independence: World's largest oil exporter; OPEC+ swing producer with 17% of global proven reserves
economy.sanctions_resilience: Low to moderate — oil price dependent; Vision 2030 diversification still nascent
economy.war_financing_capacity: High while oil above $60/barrel; breaks down rapidly below that threshold
economy.industrial_mobilization: Very low — no significant domestic defense manufacturing

countries.notes: Yemen war is the definitive evidence of military limitations despite Western hardware: bought the equipment, lacked the doctrine and institutional culture to use it effectively. Oil lever remains primary strategic instrument.

countries.name: Turkey

key_capabilities: Bayraktar TB2 and Akinci drones: combat-proven in Syria, Libya, Nagorno-Karabakh, Ukraine
Bosphorus/Dardanelles control under Montreux Convention
NATO's second-largest conventional army after US
Growing domestic defense industry reducing import dependence from 70% to 30%
vulnerabilities: NATO ally with independent foreign policy creates alliance management tension
Kurdish PKK insurgency remains unresolved
Economic instability (40%+ inflation 2022-2023) degrades procurement capacity
Russian gas dependency — leverage cuts both ways

economy.energy_independence: Net energy importer; major Russian gas pipeline transit hub
economy.sanctions_resilience: Moderate — large diversified economy, trades with both Russia and West
economy.war_financing_capacity: Moderate — structural inflation constrains sustained defense spending
economy.industrial_mobilization: Moderate and rising — defense exports grew $1B (2012) to $6B (2023)
key_economic_weapons: NATO membership card as bargaining chip

alliances: Mediation role between Russia and Ukraine
countries.notes: Bosphorus control under Montreux Convention is decisive in any Black Sea conflict. Drone export program created influence across 30+ countries. Independent posture between Russia and NATO creates unique leverage — can extract concessions from both simultaneously.

countries.name: India

key_capabilities: Nuclear triad capability (land ICBM, submarine SLBM, air-delivered)
Indian Ocean naval dominance: Andaman Islands forward base
Large ground forces optimized for Himalayan high-altitude warfare
BrahMos supersonic anti-ship missile (Mach 2.8, 500km range)
S-400 Triumf air defense system
vulnerabilities: Two-front war risk: simultaneous Pakistan and China pressure
High-altitude logistics in Ladakh sector: limited all-weather road and rail
Mixed equipment (Russian + Western + indigenous) creates logistics complexity

economy.energy_independence: Major energy importer — 85% of oil imported; Russia discount oil post-2022 reduces cost
economy.sanctions_resilience: Very high — strategic autonomy doctrine; trades with Russia, US, Israel, Iran simultaneously
economy.war_financing_capacity: Moderate — large growing economy but defense only 2% GDP
economy.industrial_mobilization: Moderate and improving — Make in India defense push
key_economic_weapons: Pharmaceutical generics (60% of global generic drugs)

countries.notes: Strategic autonomy doctrine: simultaneously buys arms from Russia, US, France, and Israel while refusing formal alignment. Demographic dividend: 1.4B population with median age 28.7 provides 20-30 year manpower advantage over aging China (median 39) and Russia (median 40).

countries.name: North Korea

key_capabilities: Nuclear arsenal with ICBM delivery (Hwasong-17, range 15,000km — continental US range)
Largest artillery concentration in the world: 8,000+ guns within range of Seoul
Chemical and biological weapons stockpile (estimated 2,500-5,000 tons chemical)
Cyber capabilities (Lazarus Group) for financial theft and disruption
vulnerabilities: Near-autarkic economy dependent on China (90% of trade)
Chronic food insecurity: 42% population undernourished
Military equipment is 1970s-1980s Soviet vintage, poorly maintained
Fuel dependency on China makes sustained conventional war impossible without Beijing backing

economy.energy_independence: Critically dependent on Chinese fuel imports
economy.sanctions_resilience: Moderate — fully autarkic model; arms exports and cyber theft fund weapons program
economy.war_financing_capacity: Very low for conventional war; nuclear deterrent is the cheap substitute
economy.industrial_mobilization: Moderate in defense sector; economy fully militarized (Songun policy)
key_economic_weapons: Artillery bombardment of Seoul as hostage deterrent

alliances: De facto isolation beyond patron relationships
countries.notes: Kim regime correctly identified nuclear weapons as the only reliable deterrent to regime change — Libya and Iraq abandoned WMD programs and were overthrown. Artillery threat to Seoul (25M metro, 40km from border) creates hostage deterrent below nuclear threshold. Chinese fuel cutoff = regime collapse within months.

countries.name: Pakistan

key_capabilities: Nuclear arsenal with Nasr tactical missile (60km) — world's lowest declared nuclear threshold
China-supplied JF-17 Thunder fighters (100+ aircraft)
ISI intelligence service: sophisticated covert operations and proxy warfare
vulnerabilities: Severe fiscal crisis: IMF dependency, near-depletion foreign exchange episodes
Internal insurgency: TTP Pashtun Taliban fighting Pakistani state continuously
Civil-military imbalance: army operates as state-within-a-state
Water scarcity: Indus River dependency shared with India — existential in long conflict

economy.energy_independence: Net energy importer; severe energy sector circular debt ($15B+)
economy.sanctions_resilience: Low — IMF-dependent; Western sanctions would be rapidly crippling
economy.war_financing_capacity: Very low — war with India requires immediate Chinese financial backstop
economy.industrial_mobilization: Low-moderate — some domestic defense (POF munitions, JF-17 assembly) but largely import-dependent
key_economic_weapons: Nuclear deterrent as asymmetric equalizer against India's conventional superiority

countries.notes: Pakistan army exists primarily to fight India, not to defend the state from all threats. Nasr tactical nuclear missile explicitly lowers nuclear threshold to deter India's Cold Start conventional doctrine. ISI proxy warfare capability (Afghanistan, Kashmir) imposes costs on India below the war threshold.

countries.name: Germany

key_capabilities: Leopard 2 main battle tank (world's most exported Western MBT — 3,500 in service globally)
European Typhoon air superiority fighters
Advanced submarine force (Type 212A air-independent propulsion)
Rheinmetall defense industrial complex: Europe's largest ammunition and armor producer
vulnerabilities: Bundeswehr chronically underfunded for 30 years (post-Cold War peace dividend): equipment readiness ~50% in 2022
No nuclear deterrent; entirely dependent on US nuclear umbrella (NATO Article 5)
Russian gas dependency (55% of gas from Russia pre-2022) now severed — transition painful
Constitutional constraints (Basic Law Article 87a) historically limited out-of-area operations

economy.energy_independence: Net energy importer; pivoting from Russian gas to LNG imports (Qatar, US)
economy.sanctions_resilience: High — diverse export economy, eurozone membership, EU market access
economy.war_financing_capacity: High — low debt, Europe's largest economy, €100B special Bundeswehr fund announced 2022
economy.industrial_mobilization: Moderate-high — world-class industrial base (Volkswagen, BASF, Siemens, Rheinmetall) but defense manufacturing atrophied post-Cold War

countries.notes: Zeitenwende (historical turning point) declared February 2022 after Russian invasion: Germany pledging 2% GDP defense spending and €100B special Bundeswehr fund. Industrial capacity to rearm is enormous — Rheinmetall alone can scale artillery shell production from 70,000 to 1.4M shells/year within 3 years. The constraint is political will and speed of procurement reform, not industrial capacity.

countries.name: France

key_capabilities: Independent nuclear deterrent (Force de Frappe) — only EU nuclear power post-Brexit
Charles de Gaulle aircraft carrier (sole non-US nuclear-powered carrier outside US)
Rafale multirole fighter (proven in Libya, Mali, Syria, Iraq operations)
Foreign Legion (6,000 professionals) — elite rapid deployment force
SCALP/Storm Shadow cruise missiles (1,000km range)
Operation Barkhane-type expeditionary capability in Africa
vulnerabilities: Small professional army for a major power (203,000 active)
Aging nuclear arsenal requiring expensive modernization (SNV submarine refresh)
Africa operations failed: expelled from Mali (2022), Burkina Faso (2023), Niger (2023) — Wagner replaced France
Domestic political fragmentation (far-right/far-left growth) constrains overseas commitments

economy.energy_independence: 70% nuclear electricity generation (57 reactors) — low carbon exposure; oil importer
economy.sanctions_resilience: High — eurozone, nuclear power, UNSC veto, diverse exports
economy.war_financing_capacity: Moderate — high debt constrains surge spending; nuclear arsenal maintenance competes with conventional
economy.industrial_mobilization: Moderate — MBDA missiles, Thales electronics, Naval Group submarines; but small manufacturing base
key_economic_weapons: Nuclear technology exports (60+ reactors contracted globally)

countries.notes: Only EU nuclear power post-Brexit — France's Force de Frappe is Europe's independent deterrent if US commitment wavers. Africa expulsion (Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger 2022-2023) to Wagner is the most significant French strategic defeat since Algeria. Macron's 'European strategic autonomy' doctrine is the French effort to reduce NATO dependence on US while retaining alliance benefits.

countries.name: Japan

key_capabilities: F-35A/B fleet (147 ordered — one of world's largest F-35 customers)
8 Aegis destroyers providing ballistic missile defense
JS Izumo and JS Kaga carriers being converted to F-35B capability
Tomahawk cruise missiles (acquired 2024 — 400 ordered, ending decades of no-strike-capability)
Advanced submarine force (22 diesel-electric submarines, world-class)
Cyber defense command established 2022, planned 4,000 personnel
vulnerabilities: Article 9 constitutional pacifism constraining reinterpretation politically
88% of oil imports transit Malacca Strait — same vulnerability as China
Extremely aging population (median age 48) limiting conscript pool
Domestic political opposition to rearmament from peace movement
Three missile threats simultaneously: China DF-21D, North Korea Nodong/Hwasong, Russia Iskander in Far East

economy.energy_independence: Major energy importer (oil and LNG); nuclear electricity rebuilding post-Fukushima
economy.sanctions_resilience: Moderate — dollar holdings, technology exports; vulnerable to energy supply disruption
economy.war_financing_capacity: Unusual: 264% debt/GDP but almost entirely domestic (no foreign debt vulnerability); JGB market stable due to captive domestic institutional buyers
economy.industrial_mobilization: High — world-class manufacturing (Toyota, Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, IHI, Kawasaki) with rapid conversion potential
key_economic_weapons: Semiconductor equipment exports (ASML-level control of lithography chemicals/coatings via JSR, Shin-Etsu)
World's largest creditor nation ($3.5T net foreign assets)

alliances: US-Japan Security Treaty (Article 5 applies, 54,000 US troops in Japan)
countries.notes: Doubling defense budget 2023-2027 (1% → 2% GDP) is the largest Japanese rearmament since WWII. Tomahawk acquisition is strategically significant — Japan now has counter-strike capability for first time since 1945. Taiwan strait vital: 88% of Japanese oil transits past Taiwan. North Korea ballistic missile tests (2017, 2022-2023 overflights of Japan) drove public support for rearmament to historic highs.

countries.name: South Korea

key_capabilities: K2 Black Panther main battle tank (arguably world's most advanced MBT — 1,000 ordered by Poland)
K9 Thunder self-propelled howitzer (world's best-selling — sold to 10+ countries)
F-35A/B fleet and indigenous KF-21 Boramae 4.5-gen fighter in development
3 KDX-III Aegis destroyers with SM-3 ballistic missile defense

Samsung/Hanwha defense industrial complex producing world-competitive platforms

K-defense export success creates geopolitical complications (Poland, Romania, Australia relationships)

No independent nuclear deterrent; relies on US extended deterrence (nuclear umbrella)

economy.energy_independence: Major energy importer; world's 3rd largest LNG importer
economy.sanctions_resilience: Moderate — export-dependent economy (40% exports/GDP) vulnerable to trade disruption
economy.war_financing_capacity: High — moderate debt, disciplined fiscal policy, large forex reserves ($420B)
economy.industrial_mobilization: Very high — Samsung, Hyundai, POSCO, LG can pivot rapidly; semiconductor fabs are dual-use
key_economic_weapons: Semiconductor manufacturing (Samsung Foundry, SK Hynix — 30%+ of global DRAM/NAND)
Defense exports ($17B in 2022 — world's 9th largest exporter)

alliances: US-ROK Mutual Defense Treaty (US provides nuclear umbrella)
countries.notes: K-defense export success is transforming South Korea into a mid-tier geopolitical power beyond its size. TFR 0.72 (2023) is the world's lowest — the demographic cliff will reduce military-age population 40% by 2040, making near-term defense investment critical. North Korea artillery threat to Seoul is the asymmetric deterrent that makes pre-emptive conventional war extremely costly for both sides.

countries.name: United Arab Emirates

key_capabilities: F-16 Block 60 Desert Falcon (most advanced F-16 variant ever built — better than US own)
Bayraktar TB2 and Wing Loong II drone fleets, combat-proven in Libya and Yemen
Mirage 2000-9 fleet with MICA missiles
Yemen combat experience (2015-2019): UAE forces most effective Arab military in field
Sovereign wealth fund ($1.4T combined: ADIA, Mubadala, ADQ) as geopolitical instrument

No strategic depth — entire country within Iranian missile range
Dubai financial hub vulnerable to Iranian disruption (proximity to Hormuz)
Dependent on US security umbrella for existential deterrence

economy.energy_independence: Major oil exporter; ADNOC controls $800B+ in oil assets
economy.sanctions_resilience: High — Dubai is global sanctions-busting hub; trades with Iran, Russia, and West simultaneously
economy.war_financing_capacity: Very high — sovereign wealth funds ($1.4T) can finance prolonged engagement without touching GDP
economy.industrial_mobilization: Low domestically; but SWF can purchase defense capacity globally
key_economic_weapons: Sovereign Wealth Fund ($1.4T) as geopolitical investment tool
Dubai as global financial and trade hub

alliances: China major trading partner and BRI node
countries.notes: Abraham Accords made UAE-Israel the most consequential secret intelligence partnership in the Middle East — sharing targeting data on Iran is the unspoken core of the agreement. UAE sovereign wealth funds ($1.4T) give economic leverage far exceeding GDP. Yemen operation (2015-2019) proved UAE military effectiveness — UAE forces performed better than Saudi coalition partners and withdrew strategically when objectives were met.

countries.name: Egypt

key_capabilities: 3rd largest army in Middle East: 1,360 M1A1 Abrams tanks (assembled in Egypt under US license)
F-16 fleet (220+ aircraft) plus new Rafale fighters (30 delivered, 24 more on order)
Suez Canal control — $9.4B/year revenue and global transit chokepoint
Large submarine and corvette fleet in Mediterranean/Red Sea

Internal Islamist insurgency (Sinai Province/ISIS affiliate) ongoing low-level conflict
Military equipment mix (US + Russian + French) creates maintenance and interoperability complexity

economy.energy_independence: Gas self-sufficient (Zohr field discovery 2015); LNG exporter
economy.sanctions_resilience: Moderate — Gulf financial patron backing (Saudi/UAE deposits); IMF backstop
economy.war_financing_capacity: Low — fiscal crisis endemic; any war requires immediate Gulf financial support
economy.industrial_mobilization: Moderate — domestic military industries (ammunition, armored vehicles, ships) at Arab level
key_economic_weapons: Suez Canal closure ($9.4B/year revenue; 15% of global trade hostage)
Nile water control vs. upstream Ethiopia

alliances: US major non-NATO ally ($1.3B annual military aid)
Peace treaty with Israel (Camp David 1979 — enduring)
Russia (Rafale competitor; also buying Russian weapons)
countries.notes: Camp David peace treaty with Israel has endured 45+ years — the most durable Arab-Israeli diplomatic achievement. GERD dam (Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam) is Egypt's first existential water security threat: Nile provides 97% of freshwater for 105M Egyptians. Military coup tradition (1952, 2013) makes civil-military relations the decisive internal variable — the army controls 40% of the formal economy.

countries.name: Brazil

key_capabilities: Largest military in South America
Amazon surveillance system (SIPAM/SISFRON) protecting world's largest forest
Nuclear submarine program (Álvaro Alberto) in development — hull complete, reactor pending
Embraer defense aircraft (KC-390 military transport competing with C-130)
UN peacekeeping tradition (MINUSTAH Haiti command, MONUC)
